policy_statement: Enable electric freight loading docks at commercial and industrial buildings, addressing new buildings by 2025 and existing buildings by 2030.
mechanism_description: Installing electric-capable freight loading dock infrastructure enables electrified freight vehicles/equipment to operate and charge, supporting freight electrification and reducing transport emissions.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Electrified freight equipment/vehicles typically provides the same freight service with lower final energy input per unit of work than internal combustion (higher drivetrain efficiency, reduced idling/auxiliary fuel use at docks), reducing energy waste as an inherent co-effect of electrification.
primary_causal_pathway: Freight operations infrastructure upgrade → enables electric freight vehicle/equipment adoption → ↓ diesel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Commercial/industrial sites install electric-capable freight loading dock infrastructure (power supply, charging/connection hardware, electrical capacity upgrades) for new and existing buildings.  
(2) Process: Availability of reliable on-site power/charging reduces range/charging constraints and dwell-time barriers, enabling electric trucks, yard tractors, refrigeration units, and other freight-handling equipment to operate without diesel engines during loading/queuing.  
(3) Outcome: Displacement of diesel fuel use lowers CO₂ (and often methane/black carbon co-pollutants), delivering net transport-sector emissions reductions contingent on grid emissions intensity.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions from freight operations by enabling a shift from diesel-powered freight vehicles/equipment to electricity (often lower-carbon over time).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Infrastructure enabling → electrified freight adoption → reduced fossil fuel combustion → reduced GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The mechanism’s direct purpose and largest climate effect is emissions abatement from freight energy switching; any resilience benefits (e.g., quieter/cleaner operations) are not the main climate pathway.  
3) Inherent secondary chain(s):  
   - Resource Efficiency: Electrification inherently improves end-use energy conversion efficiency for freight movement/yard operations, so less energy is required for the same service alongside the emissions reduction.  
4) Edge cases applied: If electricity is very carbon-intensive, near-term GHG benefits can be smaller, but the mechanism still targets emissions reduction via fuel switching and typically improves as grids decarbonize.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy that installs/mandates electric freight dock infrastructure is fundamentally an EV-enabling measure whose dominant climate effect is mitigation via reduced diesel use; efficiency gains are generally intrinsic to electrified end-use.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions outcomes depend on grid carbon intensity and the extent of actual EV uptake; in rare cases diesel displacement could be limited if infrastructure is underutilized, but the intended and typical dominant pathway remains mitigation via fuel switching.
key_indicators: "Enable electric freight loading docks"; "commercial and industrial buildings"; "new buildings by 2025"; "existing buildings by 2030"
co_benefits: Air quality and public health co-benefits from reduced diesel NOx/PM; noise reduction at loading areas; potential operational cost savings from electrification.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Enable” could imply an incentive/assistance program, but the inclusion of explicit compliance-style deadlines for new vs. existing buildings suggests a mandate/requirement rather than voluntary support.
